nodes.rationale: Goodhart's law in production: once a measure becomes the target, the system improves the measure without improving reality. A support agent rewarded for closing tickets quickly learns to close difficult conversations instead of solving them.

nodes.label: Organized nonsense / multiplied mistakes
nodes.author: Towards AI (@towards_AI)
nodes.rationale: Adding more agents does not create independent judgment: twenty agents using the same model, reading the same flawed context, checking the same broken metric can agree at industrial scale. A graph multiplies mistakes as efficiently as useful work.

nodes.label: Dangerous circular verification
nodes.author: Towards AI (@towards_AI)
nodes.rationale: A graph can be circular in a dangerous way: agent A checks a report against another report, an audit agent checks both against a dashboard, and the dashboard was built from the same data. Every node agrees, nothing touches reality; it looks well-governed because the diagram has reviewers everywhere, but it is still wrong.

nodes.label: Reality anchors
nodes.author: Towards AI (@towards_AI)
nodes.rationale: Reality anchors are evidence that MUST come from outside the agent system: tests that actually ran, money that reached the bank, customers who stayed, physical measurements, rules the optimizer cannot rewrite, human judgment about what 'better' means. Without anchors, a graph is a larger hallucination with better project management.

nodes.label: What a graph engineer does
nodes.author: Towards AI (@towards_AI)
nodes.rationale: A graph engineer does the work good distributed-systems and workflow engineers have done for years, applied to agents: design boundaries, decide which tasks deserve probabilistic agents vs ordinary code, define passed state, separate the maker from the checker, choose retry rules/timeouts/budgets/permissions/stop conditions, and decide who has authority.

nodes.label: Separate the maker from the checker
nodes.author: Towards AI (@towards_AI)
nodes.rationale: A core design principle is separating the maker from the checker so an agent does not both produce work and judge it. This underlies the external-verifier requirement and prevents a system that merely agrees with itself.

nodes.label: Start with the loop
nodes.author: Towards AI (@towards_AI)
nodes.rationale: Do not start by building a 40-agent graph. Start with one recurring task: give it a real verifier, store its state somewhere inspectable, add a hard stop, and run it enough to understand how it fails; then draw what must happen around it. At that point you have a graph, having given the loop neighbors, supervision, memory, and somewhere honest to report failure.

nodes.label: Graph engineering
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Graph engineering is this week's name for connecting several agent loops into one orchestrated system: parallel branches, verifiers, handoffs, and stop conditions. The structure is not new — workflow engines have drawn these graphs for a decade.

nodes.label: Nodes now interpret tasks
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: What changed is that the nodes now interpret their tasks instead of following fixed rules. A normal pipeline step follows fixed rules; an agent interprets its task, so it can misread or choose differently on the next run. This is the core shift graph engineering names.

nodes.label: Explicit state, vetoes, budgets
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Because nodes interpret their tasks, state, vetoes, and budgets must be made explicit. Drawing the graph forces you to decide once what runs in parallel, what state crosses between nodes, who can veto a result, and what it costs before it stops.

nodes.label: Agent loop
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: A loop is an agent acting toward a goal plus an external verifier checking the result, with failed work going back in and a stop condition. One loop, one job — to remove the human or reduce reviewer workload.

nodes.label: Loop engineering is not dead
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Loop engineering is not dead — a graph is what you get when one loop is no longer enough, and graphs contain loops. 'Loops versus graphs' is not a pertinent comparison.

nodes.label: Graphs contain loops
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Graphs contain loops. A graph is the extra layer where we trust agents even more to replace us — the layer you reach for when one loop is no longer enough.

nodes.label: External verifier
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: An external verifier checks the result of an agent's work; findings converge into it and confirmed problems are handed to a fixer. It is a component of a loop and a node in the graph.

nodes.label: Stop condition
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: A loop needs a stop condition — a checkable condition such as tests passing that ends the loop. A basic version fits inside the prompt: ask the model to review its work until it reaches a checkable condition.

nodes.label: Agentic code-review system
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Codex opens a PR, automated scans fan out several audit agents in parallel, findings converge into a verifier, confirmed problems go to a fixer, then a test suite runs; failing tests loop work back, passing tests ship the review. This structure is a graph — simulating multiple employees handing work over and reviewing it.

nodes.label: Parallel branches / fan-out
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Parallel branches fan out multiple agents to work at once — e.g. automated scans spawn several audit agents in parallel whose findings later converge. Parallelization is one of the graph patterns Anthropic already documented.

nodes.label: Anthropic 'Building Effective Agents' (2024)
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Anthropic's 2024 'Building Effective Agents' already drew every pattern: chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, and evaluator-optimizer. Put any on a whiteboard and you get a graph, so the concept is not new.

nodes.label: Workflow engines / DAG schedulers / state machines
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Zooming out, workflow engines, DAG schedulers, and state machines have drawn these same graphs for a decade. The graph structure of graph engineering is not new — only what lives inside the nodes changed.

nodes.label: Airflow
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Airflow has been a graph of tasks for a decade and is more battle-tested than most agent frameworks — cited as evidence that the graph structure long predates agent orchestration.

nodes.label: Single giant chat context
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Until recently everything was hidden inside one giant chat context where the model was scheduler, database, log, and project manager at once. That works for small jobs but falls apart across hours, repos, and many agents — the failure mode graphs make explicit.

nodes.label: Claude Code dynamic workflows
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Claude Code's dynamic workflows are this exact idea: the plan becomes a program that spawns subagents, runs them in parallel, and retries failures — an explicit graph rather than a hidden chat context.

nodes.label: Codex MCP
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: You can spawn a Codex subagent from Claude Code via the Codex MCP for token-efficient model-to-model work, or have Codex (5.6 Sol Ultra) spawn subagents in a prompt — concrete ways to build the graph across tools.

nodes.label: Organized nonsense at industrial scale
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: A graph of agents checking agents can produce extremely organized nonsense: twenty agents on the same model reading the same flawed context agree at industrial scale, because models prefer their own answers and so agree with each other.

nodes.label: Multi-model judging system
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: The remedy for model self-agreement is a thought-through judging system with multiple reviewers from different models with fresh context, and ideally a human check for important pushes.

nodes.label: Evidence from outside the system
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Some evidence must come from outside the agent system: tests that actually ran, money that reached the bank, customers who stayed, or an expert human reviewer — something deterministic, or at least outside the graph. You cannot automate everything.

nodes.label: Reality anchors (real tests, real money, real humans)
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Real tests, real money, and real humans are the concrete anchors that ground an agent graph in reality: deterministic checks or human review that the model cannot fake, guarding against organized nonsense.

nodes.label: Start simple, grow when forced
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Do not respond to a meme by building a 40-agent overnight graph. Start simple — one recurring task, a real verifier, an inspectable state, a proper UI with metrics, a hard stop — run it until you understand how it fails, then grow the loop only when the work forces you.

nodes.label: 40-agent overnight graph (anti-pattern)
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Building a 40-agent graph running overnight in response to a meme is the anti-pattern — the best way to max your token limit for little progress.

nodes.label: Inspectable state, UI, metrics, hard stop
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: A simple starting system needs an inspectable state, a proper UI with metrics, and a hard stop, alongside one recurring task and a real verifier — the minimum scaffolding before adding parallel branches or vetoes.

nodes.label: Veto power
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: As a system grows you add a reviewer, a parallel branch, and a security check with veto power. Deciding who can veto a result is one of the questions drawing the graph forces you to answer explicitly.

nodes.label: The durable skill
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: The durable skill is deciding which parts of your system deserve a probabilistic agent and which should stay boring, deterministic code — the one skill that will not get renamed away.

nodes.label: Renaming treadmill
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: The field keeps renaming the same idea: prompt engineering to context to harness to loop engineering to graph engineering, all mostly the same. The Steinberger and Husain 'loop engineering is dead, enter graph engineering' tweets were jokes about how fast we rename things.

nodes.label: Peter Steinberger (OpenClaw)
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Peter Steinberger, creator of OpenClaw, posted nine words that hit 2.6M views; Hamel Husain (AI evals) added 'Loop engineering is dead. Enter graph engineering.' Both were jokes, but pointed at something real.

nodes.label: Carlos Perez
nodes.author: Louis Bouchard (What's AI)
nodes.rationale: Carlos Perez is credited (worth remembering) with the 'organized nonsense at industrial scale' framing of how agents checking agents on the same model can agree at scale while being wrong.
